Scientists discover high levels of microplastics in human blood

By Victoria Gill 12 March 2024

Scientists have found microplastics in human blood for the first time, raising concerns about the impact of plastic pollution on human health.

A study of 22 anonymous blood samples found plastic particles in 17 participants. The most common plastic was PET, used in drinks bottles, followed by polystyrene, used in food packaging.

"This is a breakthrough finding," said Professor Dick Vethaak, an ecotoxicologist at Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam. "We have now proven that our blood, our body, is no longer plastic-free."

What are microplastics?

Microplastics are tiny plastic fragments less than 5mm in size. They come from the breakdown of larger plastic products, as well as from synthetic clothing fibers and tire dust.

The particles can enter the body through food, water, and air. Previous studies have found them in the lungs, liver, and placenta of humans.

"The plastic particles can carry chemicals that may cause inflammation and cellular damage."

— Professor Dick Vethaak

Health implications

While the health effects of microplastics in human blood are not yet fully understood, researchers say the findings are concerning.

The study, published in the journal Environment International, used a technique to detect particles as small as 0.0007mm.

"We need to fund further research to understand what this means for human health," said Dr. Fay Couceiro, a biogeochemist at the University of Portsmouth.
